pattern: Multiple sclerotic foci in the pelvis

findings.dominantFinding: The dominant imaging finding is multiple ovoid, well-defined sclerotic foci scattered throughout the pelvis. These small dense bone islands are typically symmetrically distributed and clustered around the articular regions.
findings.supportingFeatures: Supporting features include the absence of any associated bony expansion, cortical destruction, or periosteal reaction. Similar sclerotic foci are frequently seen in the epiphyseal and metaphyseal regions of long and short tubular bones.
findings.criticalNegatives: Importantly, there is no evidence of aggressive features such as permeative bone destruction or an associated soft tissue mass. Furthermore, there is no coarsening of the trabecular pattern or cortical thickening to suggest Paget disease.
findings.influentialFeature: The feature that most influences my thinking is the symmetric, periarticular distribution of small, well-defined ovoid sclerotic foci without any aggressive features or bone destruction.

differentials.primaryDiagnosis: The appearances are most in keeping with Osteopoikilosis, given the characteristic symmetric, ovoid, well-defined sclerotic foci resembling multiple bone islands. This is an asymptomatic, benign skeletal sclerotic dysplasia that predominantly affects the pelvis, carpal and tarsal bones, and the ends of tubular bones.
differentials.principleDifferential: The principle differential would be Metastatic disease, however osteoblastic metastases from prostate or breast cancer typically present as ill-defined, asymmetric lesions of varying sizes that may involve the axial skeleton more diffusely. Furthermore, osteopoikilosis lesions are characteristically centered around the joints and are uniform in their dense appearance.
differentials.excludeDiagnosis: A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Paget disease, given it frequently affects the pelvis in older adults and missing sarcomatous degeneration could be catastrophic. However, Paget disease is characterized by cortical thickening, osseous expansion, and coarse trabeculation, rather than discrete tiny sclerotic dots.

management.priority: From a radiological perspective, the priority is to confirm the benign, incidental nature of these findings and reassure the patient, avoiding unnecessary invasive biopsies.
management.priorImaging: I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is crucial to confirm that these sclerotic foci are long-standing and stable, further supporting a benign skeletal dysplasia over active metastatic disease.
management.furtherImaging: I would recommend plain radiographs of the hands or feet if current imaging is insufficient, as demonstrating similar symmetric periarticular sclerotic foci in the short tubular bones definitively clinches the diagnosis of osteopoikilosis.
management.nuclearMedicine: If diagnostic uncertainty remains and metastatic disease cannot be completely excluded clinically, a technetium-99m bone scan could be utilised, which typically shows normal activity in osteopoikilosis but intensely increased uptake in metastatic or Pagetic bone.
management.mdtDiscussion: This should go to the Musculoskeletal MDT. My report would clearly document the classic symmetric distribution and benign features of the sclerotic foci to confidently establish the diagnosis of osteopoikilosis and prevent further unnecessary oncological workup.

vivaAnswer.fullScript:
The dominant imaging finding is multiple ovoid, well-defined sclerotic foci scattered throughout the pelvis. These small dense bone islands are typically symmetrically distributed and clustered around the articular regions. Supporting features include the absence of any associated bony expansion, cortical destruction, or periosteal reaction. Similar sclerotic foci are frequently seen in the epiphyseal and metaphyseal regions of long and short tubular bones. Importantly, there is no evidence of aggressive features such as permeative bone destruction or an associated soft tissue mass. Furthermore, there is no coarsening of the trabecular pattern or cortical thickening to suggest Paget disease. The feature that most influences my thinking is the symmetric, periarticular distribution of small, well-defined ovoid sclerotic foci without any aggressive features or bone destruction.

The appearances are most in keeping with Osteopoikilosis, given the characteristic symmetric, ovoid, well-defined sclerotic foci resembling multiple bone islands. This is an asymptomatic, benign skeletal sclerotic dysplasia that predominantly affects the pelvis, carpal and tarsal bones, and the ends of tubular bones. The principle differential would be Metastatic disease, however osteoblastic metastases from prostate or breast cancer typically present as ill-defined, asymmetric lesions of varying sizes that may involve the axial skeleton more diffusely. Furthermore, osteopoikilosis lesions are characteristically centered around the joints and are uniform in their dense appearance. A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Paget disease, given it frequently affects the pelvis in older adults and missing sarcomatous degeneration could be catastrophic. However, Paget disease is characterized by cortical thickening, osseous expansion, and coarse trabeculation, rather than discrete tiny sclerotic dots.

From a radiological perspective, the priority is to confirm the benign, incidental nature of these findings and reassure the patient, avoiding unnecessary invasive biopsies. I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is crucial to confirm that these sclerotic foci are long-standing and stable, further supporting a benign skeletal dysplasia over active metastatic disease. I would recommend plain radiographs of the hands or feet if current imaging is insufficient, as demonstrating similar symmetric periarticular sclerotic foci in the short tubular bones definitively clinches the diagnosis of osteopoikilosis. If diagnostic uncertainty remains and metastatic disease cannot be completely excluded clinically, a technetium-99m bone scan could be utilised, which typically shows normal activity in osteopoikilosis but intensely increased uptake in metastatic or Pagetic bone. This should go to the Musculoskeletal MDT. My report would clearly document the classic symmetric distribution and benign features of the sclerotic foci to confidently establish the diagnosis of osteopoikilosis and prevent further unnecessary oncological workup.
